item.content:encoded: Wow, I must have missed when this broke through the 100 mark. Yay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… audio that invokes machine depression, or possibly the version of what would later become known as Drapetomania (SCP-1851-EX) amongst American slave owners as experienced by the early Jews in Egypt and Babylon… with some direct links to such. Machine religion/slavery?

Anyway, I just hope Huber had the presence of mind to reach over and yank the power cable out of that computer…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is Alex referencing verses 5-6 here? Did she forget her Jerusalem?

I didn't have that in mind, I'll admit, although I quite like that interpretation. With Alexandra, what I was going for was the feeling of depression (that "brokenness"), as interpreted by a machine intelligence. So really Alexandra is evoking verse 1 of the psalm, if anything. That also means that the three interviews progress through the psalm in reading order - of course, the psalm has so many good lines that I would have loved to play with additional interviews to riff on them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't recall Psalm 137 before reading this article. After reading, I found the psalm. I liked that 2412 and 629 specifically evoked sections of the psalm (respectively, verses 3-4 with hints of 7-9, and verses 7-9 more explicitly).

What I'm curious about:

Alexandra: I don't know. I've never had this sensation - like I've lost information, like a huge deletion where code should be. I can feel where it should go. Like I'm broken.

Is Alex referencing verses 5-6 here? Did she forget her Jerusalem?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow - thanks for this, mlister. I had read up on Hayes for the skip, but somehow never learned that his version was in 4 parts. Because my headcanon is that the music box plays the most commonly known version of the tune (which is a 3-part round), I've adjusted the Description to say that it's the 3-part round *based on* Hayes' version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2519 is a digital audio file named "Babylon.mp3", which encodes the 3-part round "By the waters of Babylon", written by Philip Hayes (1738-1797) based on the text of Psalm 137.

I've done some research, and it appears that Philip Hayes' rendition of Psalm 137 is actually a four part round. Might want to fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent use of 2412!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly, I'm confronted with the fact that I'm having a hard time getting used to this whole crosslinking "there is a canon" thing… Directly referencing the events of a tale in an article feels horribly wrong. I don't like it, I don't want to see it.

Firstly, thanks for only no-voting - I can imagine feeling like downvoting something that feels so wrong.

I'd be really interested to hear about why the crosslinking feels wrong - either here or on the Grand Crosslinking thread. This is all intended to be very loose, and I'm certainly not trying to tie down a canon. If you asked me whether the AIAD stuff and the Little Misters happened to the same Foundation, I would probably tell you no, despite what this article says!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did have a look at both of those as options, ARD - promise! But PL-76 is less sad than philosophical, and Bard is an AI, not a robot, so :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2631 is a postsapient digital intelligence with an interest in music. If you could somehow get this through its defenses this would be probably very bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you considered cross testing with SCP-2058? Once it understands the effects, it can recommend all sorts of new experiments to run!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered Jeffrey (SCP-2045) as a candidate for cross-tests when I was brainstorming this skip. Other options included SCP-2844, which would likely also be a bad idea!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2045 must never be crosstested with this. NK-Class scenario probable. Or a marked hostility toward molasses and the concept of producing it. One of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really takes a while to get where it's going. I have to disagree with above: the interviews, at least the last two, really do sound repetitive. It's a song that makes machines sad, okay, I get it.

Mostly, I'm confronted with the fact that I'm having a hard time getting used to this whole crosslinking "there is a canon" thing. I still remember the AIAD stuff clearly, so it wasn't hard to follow Alex's interview, and you've got the link there so anyone unfamiliar with it can catch up, but… Directly referencing the events of a tale in an article feels horribly wrong. I don't like it, I don't want to see it.

I think no vote is my best course of action.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole concept of an .mp3 that drives machines to tears made me smile. Oh, and that Mr. Brass sure became one nasty bugger when he got his emotional buttons pressed.

It's a bit barebones, and in reference to what you've stated above, author, it does become far too obvious far too quickly that the interviews are designed exclusively to wring emotion from the reader. With little else to back up the rather blunt deployment of the feels-bombs in the final interview, I can't bring myself to vote either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's my problem with this article as well. The anomaly isn't that remarkable and by the end for the first interview, we pretty much know what it does to machines and AIs, if the song title didn't give it away. Maybe a little bit more information about the anomaly would be nice? History or more addendums could do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Conspicuous cough

Even more conspicuous cough

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pleased that those lines worked for you. That entire interview is a blatant exercise in reader manipulation leading up to the last few lines, and I agree that it's more directly successful than either of the other two. Possibly helped by the fact that Alex is the best-known of the three characters? I'd encourage you to read 2412, which is pretty darn fine.

I agree that the anomaly is not the driving force of the article. It does have a layer or two of depth, but they're not that obvious. Which is fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the testing logs felt very true to their characters as well

To be fair, I took some liberties with Mr Brass in particular, and all three of them are acting out-of-character due to the anomaly. But I'm pleased that it felt that way, as otherwise it takes the point out of doing the crosslinks.

I was actually surprised at how few properly sad robots we have on the site - Cassandra bot is one of the few.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The actual anomaly is not that interesting, and largely hinges on the feels generated by the interviews. Mr. Brass I dont much care about, one way or the other, and 2412 I do not know enough about, but the Alex interview got me. Actually, I didn't much care for that interview either, absent the last three lines. But them last three lines… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a complete sucker for sad AI/robots in fiction and this was no exception. Not only was this a good article on its own, but the testing logs felt very true to their characters as well. As a fan of the AIAD canon, I really can't upvote enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of your great strengths as a writer is your characterization, psul, and this SCP is no different. It was chilling to read about the effects on the AIs, and how frightful it all is.

Depression is the sort of thing I wouldn't wish on my worst enemy, and one thing we don't really think about is how we feel that machines, computers, and the like, because of their construction, are immune to this sort of psychological ravaging. To make us ponder the question What if they weren't immune? tells us just how horrendously unjust it feels. It hurts in ways we didn't know we had.

Excellent work, psul. A very easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks very much, Lackwit - I'm pleased you enjoyed it. It was absolutely my aim to (a) keep a narrative progression through the three interviews; and (b) make each interview a narrative progression from the relevant skip's existing story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I figured I would save the AIAC crosslink until Alexandra herself was introduced in the second collapsible - unless there is a separate Skynet page I'm not aware of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I realized you cross-tested with three other items my heart sank. However, you handled it beautifully! The tests didn't come across as repetitive and each AI's personality really came through.
You took a potentially humdrum concept and elevated it nimbly. My heart breaks for our machine companions in captivity. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the spirit of The Grand Crosslinking: you missed an opportunity to link to the Skynet tale hub at their first mention.

+1, BTW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a fair question, and something in the Description needs to be fixed. I had imagined lyrics being generated separately by machine intelligences, but I don't think that's particularly helpful. I'll cut the reference to lyrics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confused: if the music on the mp3 file is generated by a music box, how does it have lyrics?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Back on 23 June, I posted a skip relating to the benefits and dangers of co-operation.

This one felt appropriate for today.

Many thanks to:

  • LurkD for blessing my use of Alexandra, and providing some helpful feedback
  • Choiwel for permission to use Cassandra Bot
  • Roget for ideas on how to make his Mr. Brass work on the page
  • Hercules Rockefeller, Aiden Eldritch and A Random Day for feedback, as well as Corvid and everyone else who gave this a read in chat
